A brand audit prepared in advance of the Chicago portfolio review. One of six AWC operating companies addressed in this series.
Page 01
02 · State of the Brand Today

The oldest customer relationships in the portfolio. The least story told.

Page 02

Laystrom has supplied commercial doors, frames, and hardware to the Chicago construction trade since 1951. Seventy-four years of relationships with architects, general contractors, developers, and end users. None of that is visible on the website.

The current site is a Squarespace template with stock photography, marketing copy that could describe any hardware supplier in any city, and no named customers, projects, or testimonials. A buyer landing here meets a vendor, not a partner with three generations of institutional knowledge.

What works.

Longevity
Seventy-four years in business. The single most durable trust signal in the portfolio. Most commercial hardware competitors are less than half this age.
National Distribution
The site states “Supplying Quality Products throughout the United States,” meaning Laystrom has built a distribution capability beyond its Chicago base. That is a real operational asset.
Specification Depth
Commercial door and hardware specification is a discipline most architects and GCs outsource. Laystrom is one of the firms they outsource it to. That expertise is the actual product.

What does not.

No History
The 1951 founding date appears nowhere prominent. Three generations of ownership, four decades of major Chicago projects, decades of architect relationships — none of it on the site.
No Customers Named
Zero project case studies. Zero named architects. Zero general contractors quoted. A 74-year-old commercial supplier should have a wall of relationships. Laystrom's website has a stock photo of an office.
No Specification Voice
The single most valuable thing Laystrom sells is specification expertise — knowing which hardware codes apply, which manufacturers ship on time, which finishes hold up in which environments. None of that knowledge is published, taught, or visible on the site.
03 · Historical Wins

The wins exist in institutional memory. They have never reached the public.

Page 03

Laystrom’s current marketing materials do not surface specific customer projects. The audit recommends the company’s internal records, project archives, and senior team interviews as the source for what follows. The categories below are the most likely places those wins live.

Decades of Repeat Relationships

Architects & Specifiers

The longest tail of trust in the trade

Commercial door and hardware specification is a relationship business. The architects who specify Laystrom on one project specify Laystrom on the next. Three generations of relationship continuity is a moat no new entrant can replicate — and exactly the kind of story that wins the next thirty years of work.

General Contractor Trust

Major Chicago GCs

Including the broader Leopardo network

Laystrom likely supplies several of the largest commercial general contractors in Chicago, including projects connected to Leopardo Construction itself. These relationships are operational reality but invisible to the market. Surfacing them transforms Laystrom from “a Chicago hardware supplier” to “the supplier the major Chicago GCs already trust.”

Vertical Project Depth

Healthcare · Hospitality · Education

Where door hardware is hardest to specify

Commercial doors get exponentially harder to specify in regulated environments — hospitals require specific fire ratings, hotels require ADA compliance and aesthetic finishes, schools require security and durability. Laystrom’s 74 years of work almost certainly include hundreds of these projects. Each one is a defensible case study waiting to be told.

The first 90 days of the engagement should include a structured interview with Laystrom’s senior team to surface the specific projects, named customers, and signature installs that the website currently omits. Until that intake is complete, the public-facing wins remain inferences. The likelihood that they exist is near-certain. The work to make them visible is the audit’s first deliverable.
04 · The Brand Gap

A trade-leader brand presenting as a small business.

Page 04

Laystrom has the credibility of a 74-year institution and the website of a five-year-old startup.

The risk is invisible to anyone inside the company. Laystrom’s real customers already know the brand. They built their relationships in person, over decades, through architects and project managers who know who to call. The gap shows up at the edges — with new architects, younger GCs, out-of-market developers, and any procurement decision that starts with a Google search. To those buyers, Laystrom looks like a small Chicago vendor, not the trusted institution it actually is.

What inherited customers see
A trusted partner with three generations of relationships, deep specification expertise, and the credibility to ship anywhere in the country. They keep buying. They keep referring.
What new buyers see
A generic Squarespace site for a Chicago door supplier with no project portfolio, no named customers, no specification depth visible. Indistinguishable from any newer competitor on the first search.
What younger architects do
Specify the supplier whose work they can see online. Laystrom’s 74 years of work compete on equal footing with a five-year-old competitor that has a better photo gallery.
The compounding cost
Every year of invisibility transfers brand equity to newer competitors who are actively building what Laystrom already has. The longer the silence, the more the next generation of buyers reaches the trade believing other suppliers are the institutional ones.
05 · Recommendations

Six moves over the first 90 days.

Page 05

The work begins with intake. The first deliverable is making invisible institutional memory legible to the market.

01

Conduct the senior team intake.

Two-hour structured interviews with Laystrom leadership and senior specifiers. Surface named projects, named customers, signature installs, and the stories that exist only in institutional memory. This is the brand archaeology that everything else depends on.

02

Activate the 1951 story.

Build a real About page anchored in the founding, the family ownership, the generational continuity. Position Laystrom as “the Chicago commercial hardware institution” rather than “a Chicago commercial hardware supplier.” The difference is one word and the entire competitive advantage.

03

Build five anchor case studies.

Once intake surfaces the projects, produce five real case studies with named customers, project photography, specification details, and architect or GC testimonials. One healthcare, one hospitality, one education, one corporate office, one civic or transit. The pattern proves the breadth of work without needing to surface every project.

04

Publish specification expertise.

Laystrom’s deepest competitive moat is specification knowledge. Publish it. Twelve articles per year covering door hardware code requirements, ADA compliance, fire ratings, finish durability, lead-time realities. The audience is small. The authority compounds. Younger architects find Laystrom’s site when they search for the answers Laystrom already has.

05

Introduce a face to the brand.

Specification work is a trust business. A senior member of Laystrom — ideally a long-tenured specifier or principal — becomes the public voice. LinkedIn presence, monthly bylines, occasional industry talks. Architects buy from the people they recognize. The brand should put a face forward, not hide behind a wordmark.

06

Adopt the AWC portfolio identity system.

Laystrom currently exists in its own visual silo, with a Squarespace template that signals scale below its actual position. Aligning typography, color, content infrastructure, and tone with the broader AWC portfolio produces immediate cohesion — and surfaces the connection to Leopardo Construction’s decades of trusted commercial work.

06 · Proposed Positioning
Specified by Chicago for three generations.
Engineered to outlast the building.
A line that names the trust earned across decades of architect and GC relationships, and the durability that makes the work compound. Works on the homepage, on a specification sheet, and at the top of every proposal that leaves the office.
